retrieved_docs.title: Pericoronitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The previously sterile space formed between the crown of a tooth and the dental follicle is exposed to intraoral microflora as the tooth erupts into the oral cavity. This small pocket surrounded by the soft tissue overlying the erupting tooth is difficult to clean. This forms an excellent environment for obligatory and facultative anaerobic bacteria to dwell. In addition, food can easily get impacted into this space, promoting bacterial growth and causing infection of the surrounding soft tissue, so-called pericoronitis. The microflora of pericoronitis is diverse and differs from pathogens that cause periodontitis. In a study of microbiota of pericoronitis, Actinomyces oris , Eikenella corrodens , Eubacterium nodatum , Fusobacterium nucleatum , Treponema denticola , and Eubacterium saburreum were present in high levels. [1] In short, pericoronitis results from bacterial overgrowth in a confined space exacerbated by poor cleanability. A factor that can exacerbate pericoronitis is mechanical trauma from the opposing dentition. As maxillary third molars erupt, they can occlude onto the operculum, overlaying the erupting mandibular third molars. Such repeated trauma can cause ulcerations and worsen the symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Etiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries are the basis for the majority of dental infections. While dental caries are not life-threatening, their presence can predispose patients to oral cavity infections. Other causes of dental infections include trauma or operative manipulation such as root canal treatment and third molar extractions. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The now infected and necrotic root canal system is a habitat that allows for the growth and establishment of a mixed, predominately anaerobic flora, which mainly grows in sessile biofilms and aggregates or co-aggregates. [6] The microbial invaders or their by-products can advance into the periapex of the root. In response, the host mounts a defensive mechanism through various cells, intracellular mediators, and humoral antibodies. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal abscesses are either directly related to periodontal disease or are independent of it, developing in periodontal pocket-free sites. Local factors that increase the risk of a periodontal abscess include invaginated tooth, root grooves, cracked tooth, and external root resorption. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary causative agent resulting in periodontal disease is the mixed bacterial colonization in the oral tissue. [2] [8] While there are other factors which act as secondary etiologic factors accelerating the propagation and development of periodontal diseases like developmental grooves, calculus, dental plaque, overhanging restorations, anatomical features like the short trunk, cervical enamel projections, systemic factors, genetic factors, smoking, and stress. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Pericoronitis
retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, pericoronitis's microbial composition is considered different from that of periodontitis. [39] Although pericoronitis can occur in any tooth, it is most commonly associated with the eruption of wisdom teeth, likely due to the posterior position of this tooth in the mouth and the deep grooves of this particular tooth. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Introduction -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Almost all dental infections begin with dental caries. Dental caries is highly prevalent - experienced by nearly 100% of adults worldwide. [4] The oral cavity is a polymicrobial environment, and extravasation of the bacteria into previously sterile oral spaces is the source of different types of dental infections, which include pulpitis, pulp necrosis, periodontitis, periapical and periodontal abscesses, necrotizing periodontal disease, and pericoronitis. Proper oral hygiene, mitigation of health risk factors, and early identification and treatment reduce the prevalence of dental infections and the risk of life-threatening complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: As this infection within the pulp cavity grows within the limited space of the tooth, it compresses the inner dentine walls, causing severe pain. This infection then tracks down through the root canal and inferiorly into the mandible or superiorly into the maxilla, depending on the location of the infected tooth. Another cause that predisposes individuals to a dental abscess is a partially erupted tooth, most commonly a wisdom tooth, where bacteria get trapped between the crown and soft tissues, causing inflammation. Other causes include genetic causes such as imperfect amelogenesis, which predisposes individuals to weakened enamel and makes them more susceptible to wear. Mechanical causes tooth grinding to break down tooth enamel. Medical conditions like Sjogren syndrome cause dry mouth, accelerating oropharyngeal microbial growth. Chemical irritants such as smoke from methamphetamine, immunosuppression arising from chemotherapy, or chronic immunosuppressive medical conditions such as HIV/AIDs can predispose individuals to dental caries. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Pericoronitis
retrieved_docs.content: Pericoronitis is an infection of the gingival tissue surrounding an erupting or partially erupted tooth. Before the eruption, the tooth's crown exists in a sterile space of the dental follicle. Eruption requires exposure through the gingiva into the oral cavity, which contains a flourishing microbial environment. A small pocket can form between the gingiva and the grooves of the erupting tooth, making it difficult to clean and creating an area where food can easily be impacted. This creates an ideal environment for bacterial overgrowth to progress to an infection of the surrounding soft tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for pericoronitis includes non-surgical and surgical treatment options. Aggressive oral hygiene with irrigation of the pericoronal space is recommended to dislodge any impacted debris. [62] Often, oral antibiotics help decrease the bacterial load in that area. [63] Surgical treatment involves removing the soft tissue layer overlying the erupting third molar called the operculum. This procedure is beneficial as it removes the deep pocket within the gingiva and makes it easier for the tooth to erupt. [64] Extraction of the involved tooth is often an acceptable treatment for pericoronitis involving third molar eruptions.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Combined periodontic-endodontic abscesses: can be categorized according to the origin of the infection into primary endodontic abscess with secondary periodontal involvement, primary periodontal abscess with secondary endodontic involvement, or a true combined periodontal-endodontic abscess. Correct diagnosis relies on collaborating the history, clinical examination, and pulp testing, and intraoral radiographs. [18] Positive pulp testing suggests periodontal origin, whereas a lack of response suggests endodontic origin. Pericoronal abscess: abscesses from a partially erupted tooth can often mimic a periodontal abscess. Clinicians should look out for vital adjacent teeth with no increased periodontal pocketing. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Periapical Abscess
retrieved_docs.content: The apex of a tooth is the area where the nerve enters the tooth through the root tip. Bacterial infections of the pulp can progress and collect in the periapical region, creating an abscess. Initially, this abscess is small and focal to the involved tooth. However, local extension of this infection can lead to serious complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Infection Control -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A study was undertaken by G Lynn Powell et al., which established a direct correlation between dental facilities and the identification of any organisms present. Sixty-seven percent of the impressions, dentures, crowns, and wax occlusion rims sampled showed the presence of microorganisms of varying pathogenicity. The identification of bacteria showed the presence of such organisms as Enterobacter cloacae , Escherichia coli , and Klebsiella oxytoca plus many others.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Epidemiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: The 3 most prevalent orofacial infections in 1 British study were periapical abscesses (14 to 25%), followed by pericoronitis (10 to 11%), with periodontal abscesses being the least common (6 to 7%). See Image. Periapical Abscess. [18] The incidence of periodontitis depends on multiple factors, including ethnicity and geographic location; while periodontitis has many subclassifications, the general prevalence is thought not to exceed 1%. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thus, we can describe apical periodontitis as an inflammatory disease due to the dynamic interaction between oral bacterial invaders and the body's defensive mechanism at the tooth apex. [7] Despite the complex host defensive system, the causative organisms are well-entrenched in the intricate and delicate root canal system, which is beyond the reach of the host immune response. [8] Therefore, apical periodontitis is not self-healing, and resolution of the disease can only result through surgical or non-surgical endodontic therapy or removal of the tooth in question. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Evaluation -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental radiographs can show the extent of the infection and help evaluate areas that are not obvious to visual evaluation. These include intraoral periapical radiographs, bitewing radiographs, and panoramic radiographs. These radiographs help to identify the extent of dental caries, the presence of a periapical abscess, alveolar bone loss concerning periodontitis, and third molar eruptions. The dental practitioner’s physical examination must confirm all radiographic evidence for a full evaluation and accurate diagnosis. CT scans and MRIs help assess deep-space infections and osteomyelitis. Cone-beam CT is becoming increasingly common in clinical settings to better visualize areas of oral infections. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin, a broad‑spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, may be contraindicated or its use may be avoided in the presence of an infection caused by unicellular organisms (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), underscoring the need to consider the specific type of pathogen when selecting antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: nature of these entities. This hierarchical knowledge assists clinicians in selecting appropriate antimicrobial prophylaxis and therapy and in understanding how specific infections fit within broader disease groups.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1651
retrieved_docs.content: The antibacterial therapeutic role is played by ciprofloxacin, indicating that ciprofloxacin functions as an antibacterial agent used to treat bacterial infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1430
retrieved_docs.content: Cefaklor and paromomycin each fulfill an antibacterial therapeutic role, serving as antibiotics that are employed to treat bacterial infections. Their shared function highlights their importance in antimicrobial therapy and underscores their relevance in managing bacterial diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2494
retrieved_docs.content: Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics constitute a class of antifungal agents that are administered systemically to treat invasive fungal infections. Both itraconazole and voriconazole are members of this triazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic triazole ring structure that confers broad-spectrum activity against a variety of pathogenic fungi. Their classification as systemic antimycotics underscores their use in managing serious fungal diseases where topical therapy is insufficient, highlighting the importance of these agents in comprehensive antifungal treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 94
retrieved_docs.content: topical anti‑infectives, a broad array of antibacterial medications, gynecological antibiotics and antifungal agents, providing a comprehensive view of their roles in infection control, dermatologic therapy, and related clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1538
retrieved_docs.content: Intestinal infection is classified as a bacterial intestinal infection (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي), indicating that the broader category of intestinal infections includes bacterial etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2824
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics is recognized as an allowed qualifier for gout and other crystal arthropathies, for intestinal infection, and for فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperuricemia). This indicates that hereditary factors may influence susceptibility to these conditions, underscoring the relevance of genetic considerations in their diagnosis, risk assessment, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1181
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFECTIVES FOR SYSTEMIC USE is a broad therapeutic class that includes both ANTIVIRALS FOR SYSTEMIC USE and antimycotics for systemic use. These subcategories represent systemic agents specifically targeting viral infections and fungal infections, respectively, and together they constitute the systemic anti‑infective armamentarium used in patient care.
